content.text: Selecting the right few-shot examples for each individual request — rather than hard-coding a fixed set in a prompt template — is one of the highest-leverage techniques available when deploying LLMs in production. This approach, called
dynamic few-shot selection

, couples an embedding-based retrieval system to the prompt assembly pipeline so that the model always sees the most contextually relevant demonstrations, regardless of how large or diverse the underlying example bank has grown.
What it is
Dynamic few-shot selection is the practice of
retrieving examples at inference time

from a curated bank of (input, output) pairs, using the current query as a retrieval signal. The retrieved examples are then inserted into the prompt context window before the query itself, teaching the model exactly how to behave for that class of input — without any fine-tuning.
The key contrast with static few-shot prompting is that the prompt template contains
placeholder slots

for examples, not the examples themselves. Each request triggers an embedding similarity search, selects k candidates, optionally re-ranks them, and assembles the final prompt on the fly. The model sees only examples that are semantically close to the query, keeping the context focused and token costs low even when the bank contains tens of thousands of pairs.

Dynamic few-shot is part of the broader in-context learning (ICL) family: the model learns the task format and reasoning pattern purely from what is shown in the context window, with no gradient updates to its weights. Dynamic selection extends ICL from a static technique to a scalable retrieval-augmented pattern.
Why it matters

Static few-shot prompts hit a wall in real deployments. A customer-support classifier might need to handle thousands of issue categories; a code-generation assistant might need examples spanning dozens of languages and frameworks. Pasting twenty representative examples into a static prompt covers only a tiny fraction of the input space. When a query falls outside those examples, the model generalizes poorly or extrapolates in the wrong direction.

Dynamic selection solves this by making the example bank unbounded. Maintainers add new pairs to the bank whenever they encounter a coverage gap — no prompt rewriting, no re-deployment of the base template. The retrieval layer automatically surfaces the right examples for each query. This decoupling is critical at scale: the product team that curates examples and the ML team that manages the LLM can operate independently.

There is also a quality effect. Semantically proximate examples prime the model toward the specific vocabulary, reasoning steps, and output format needed for that query — outperforming a generic fixed set even when the fixed set is larger. Research on ICL consistently finds that example relevance matters more than example count.
Benefits
Coverage scales with the bank, not the prompt.
Add new (input, output) pairs and they are immediately available to all downstream queries without changing the prompt template.
Relevant examples outperform larger generic sets.
A model primed with 4 well-matched examples often beats a static 20-example prompt on accuracy and output format adherence.
Lower token cost per request.
Only k examples (typically 3–8) appear in each prompt; the remaining thousands of examples in the bank cost nothing at inference time.
No fine-tuning required.
The base model is unchanged; behavior is shaped purely through the selected context, keeping model governance simple.
Continuous improvement loop.
Verified (input, output) pairs from production traffic can be appended to the bank, improving coverage over time without retraining.
Domain adaptability.
The same LLM and retrieval infrastructure can serve multiple domains by swapping or filtering the example bank — no separate fine-tuned model per domain.
Trade-offs
Retrieval latency adds to request time.
An ANN search over a large vector store adds 5–30 ms per request. This is usually acceptable but must be budgeted.
Example bank quality gates the system.
A poorly curated bank surfaces irrelevant or incorrect examples, actively degrading model output. The bank requires ongoing curation and quality review.
Embedding model mismatch.
The embedding model used at indexing time must match the one used at query time. Changing the embedding model requires re-indexing the entire bank.
Retrieval can fail on distribution shift.
If the query is from a domain not represented in the bank, the nearest neighbors may still be semantically far away, giving misleading examples.
Explainability is harder.
The set of examples that informed a given response varies per request. Reproducing a specific behavior requires logging which examples were retrieved.
Ordering effects.
LLMs are sensitive to the order of few-shot examples. A naive retrieval system that does not control ordering can inadvertently hurt performance.
How it works

The pipeline has four logical stages: indexing, retrieval, selection, and prompt assembly. Indexing is a one-time (or incremental) offline step; the remaining three stages execute at inference time for every request.
Indexing

Each example in the bank is run through an embedding model to produce a dense vector representation. The embedding captures semantic meaning: examples about similar topics or with similar structure map to nearby points in the embedding space. Vectors are stored in a vector database alongside the original (input, output) text.
The similarity metric used at search time must match the normalization applied during indexing. For normalized vectors (unit norm), cosine similarity and dot-product search are equivalent:
After normalization, finding the k nearest examples reduces to a maximum inner product search (MIPS), which approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) libraries implement efficiently at scale.
Retrieval

At inference time the user query is embedded with the same model used during indexing. The resulting query vector is submitted to the ANN index, which returns the top-k most similar stored vectors together with their similarity scores. Popular ANN algorithms include HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs) and IVF (Inverted File Index). HNSW trades memory for extremely low query latency and is a common default.
Selection and re-ranking

Raw ANN results are based purely on embedding distance. An optional re-ranking step applies additional criteria: diversity filtering (MMR — Maximal Marginal Relevance), metadata filters (date, category, author), or a light cross-encoder that scores the relevance of each candidate relative to the full query text. MMR balances relevance and diversity by iteratively selecting the candidate that maximises:

attrs.latex: \text{MMR}(q, S, R) = \arg\max_{e_i \in R \setminus S}\left[\lambda \cdot \text{sim}(q, e_i) - (1-\lambda)\max_{e_j \in S}\text{sim}(e_i, e_j)\right]

content.text: where
type
is the set of already-selected examples,
type
is the candidate pool, and
type
controls the relevance/diversity trade-off. A value of
type
gives equal weight to both objectives.
Prompt assembly
The selected examples are serialized into a structured few-shot block and prepended to the user query. The canonical layout is:

[System instruction]

Example 1:
Input: <input_1>
Output: <output_1>

Example 2:
Input: <input_2>
Output: <output_2>

...

Input: <user_query>
Output:
Example ordering matters: placing the most similar example closest to the query (immediately before 'Input: <user_query>') typically improves performance because of recency effects in attention.
Architecture

The diagram below shows a complete dynamic few-shot pipeline. The main inference path runs left to right through embedding, ANN search, optional re-ranking, and prompt assembly before the LLM call. The dashed feedback path represents an optional online loop that adds verified (query, response) pairs back to the example bank.

attrs.alt: Dynamic few-shot selection pipeline: query embedding → vector store ANN search → ranker/filter → prompt assembler → LLM → response, with optional feedback loop

content.text: The example bank is indexed offline. At inference time only the query embedding and ANN search are on the critical path; the ranker and assembler add negligible latency. The vector store can be replaced by any system that supports similarity search (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector, FAISS in-process).
When to use it
Good fit
The task has many input categories or domains that cannot be covered by a small fixed example set.
The example bank is already available (historical tickets, past interactions, labelled datasets) or can be built incrementally.
Token budget is limited — dynamic selection keeps prompts lean while maximizing coverage.
The application requires deterministic formatting or structured output, and diverse query phrasings make a static example set insufficient.
The domain drifts over time and new examples can be added without a deployment cycle.
Poor fit
The task is fully covered by 3–5 examples that apply universally — static prompting is simpler and has no retrieval latency.
The example bank does not yet exist and there is no path to building it; a zero-shot or chain-of-thought approach may be better.
Latency requirements are under 50 ms total and the additional retrieval hop cannot be accommodated.
The output space is so unconstrained (open-ended creative generation) that example-based steering is counterproductive.
How to implement

A minimal implementation requires three components: an embedding function, a vector store, and a prompt template. The steps below build a self-contained system using sentence-transformers for embeddings and FAISS as an in-process vector store.
Curate the example bank
— collect (input, output) pairs from historical data, human annotation, or LLM-assisted synthesis. Ensure diversity and correctness.
Choose an embedding model
— use a model trained on your domain if possible. General-purpose models (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2, text-embedding-3-small) work well for many tasks.
Index the bank
— embed all inputs and build an ANN index. Store the index alongside a JSON or database table mapping vector IDs to (input, output) text.
Build the retrieval function
— embed the query at runtime, search the index for top-k, optionally apply MMR or metadata filtering.
Assemble the prompt
— serialize examples into the prompt template with consistent delimiters. Order the most similar example last (closest to the query).
Call the LLM and log
— record which example IDs were retrieved alongside the response to enable debugging and quality review.

# Step-by-step implementation with sentence-transformers + FAISS
import json
import numpy as np
import faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# 1. Load example bank
with open("example_bank.json") as f:
    examples = json.load(f)   # [{"input": str, "output": str}, ...]

# 2. Embed the bank
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
inputs = [ex["input"] for ex in examples]
embeddings = model.encode(inputs, normalize_embeddings=True)   # shape (N, D)

# 3. Build FAISS index (inner-product on unit-norm = cosine)
dim = embeddings.shape[1]
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
index.add(embeddings.astype(np.float32))

# 4. Retrieve at runtime
def retrieve(query: str, k: int = 4) -> list[dict]:
    qvec = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    scores, ids = index.search(qvec, k)
    return [examples[i] for i in ids[0]]

# 5. Assemble prompt
def build_prompt(query: str, k: int = 4) -> str:
    shots = retrieve(query, k)
    blocks = []
    for ex in shots:
        blocks.append(f"Input: {ex['input']}\nOutput: {ex['output']}")
    shot_text = "\n\n".join(blocks)
    return f"{shot_text}\n\nInput: {query}\nOutput:"

# 6. Use the prompt
prompt = build_prompt("Classify this support ticket: 'My invoice is wrong'")
print(prompt)
Examples
Example 1 — minimal in-memory retriever

The following self-contained example uses numpy for cosine similarity with no external vector database. It is suitable for small banks (up to ~10 000 examples) and illustrates the retrieval mechanic with no dependencies beyond numpy.

import numpy as np

# Toy example bank (in practice, load from a file or DB)
BANK = [
    {"input": "reset my password",    "output": "account_access"},
    {"input": "invoice is incorrect", "output": "billing"},
    {"input": "my order hasn't arrived", "output": "shipping"},
    {"input": "can't log in",         "output": "account_access"},
    {"input": "wrong item delivered", "output": "shipping"},
    {"input": "charge on card I don't recognise", "output": "billing"},
]

# Dummy embedding: bag-of-words overlap (replace with a real model)
vocab = sorted({w for ex in BANK for w in ex["input"].lower().split()})
vocab_idx = {w: i for i, w in enumerate(vocab)}

def bow_embed(text: str) -> np.ndarray:
    vec = np.zeros(len(vocab))
    for w in text.lower().split():
        if w in vocab_idx:
            vec[vocab_idx[w]] += 1.0
    norm = np.linalg.norm(vec)
    return vec / norm if norm > 0 else vec

bank_vecs = np.stack([bow_embed(ex["input"]) for ex in BANK])

def retrieve(query: str, k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
    qvec = bow_embed(query)
    scores = bank_vecs @ qvec          # cosine on unit-norm vectors
    top_k = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:k]
    return [BANK[i] for i in top_k]

def build_prompt(query: str) -> str:
    shots = retrieve(query)
    lines = [f"Input: {s['input']}\nLabel: {s['output']}" for s in shots]
    return "\n\n".join(lines) + f"\n\nInput: {query}\nLabel:"

print(build_prompt("I was charged twice"))
# → billing-related examples appear first, followed by the query
Example 2 — production-grade with LangChain and Chroma

The following example uses LangChain's built-in dynamic few-shot prompt selector. It indexes the example bank in a Chroma vector store and uses SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector to retrieve examples at call time.

from langchain_core.prompts import FewShotPromptTemplate, PromptTemplate
from langchain_core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI

# Example bank
examples = [
    {"query": "reset my password",             "label": "account_access"},
    {"query": "invoice is incorrect",           "label": "billing"},
    {"query": "my order hasn't arrived",        "label": "shipping"},
    {"query": "can't log into the dashboard",   "label": "account_access"},
    {"query": "wrong item delivered",           "label": "shipping"},
    {"query": "mystery charge on my card",      "label": "billing"},
    {"query": "upgrade subscription plan",      "label": "billing"},
    {"query": "account suspended",              "label": "account_access"},
]

# Build selector backed by Chroma
selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    OpenAIEmbeddings(),
    Chroma,
    k=3,
    input_keys=["query"],
)

# Prompt template for each example
example_prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["query", "label"],
    template="Query: {query}\nLabel: {label}",
)

# Dynamic few-shot prompt template
prompt = FewShotPromptTemplate(
    example_selector=selector,
    example_prompt=example_prompt,
    suffix="Query: {query}\nLabel:",
    input_variables=["query"],
)

# At inference time: examples are selected per-query
final_prompt = prompt.format(query="payment failed on checkout")
print(final_prompt)

# Feed to the model
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
response = llm.invoke(final_prompt)
print(response.content)   # → "billing"
Real-world example

A B2B SaaS company processes 15 000 inbound support tickets per day across 200+ product categories. Their prior system used a static 20-example classifier prompt. As the product grew, routing accuracy dropped to 71 % because new categories were not represented in the fixed examples.

The team built a dynamic few-shot pipeline over their historical ticket bank (42 000 labelled tickets). They indexed all 42 000 input texts with text-embedding-3-small, stored vectors in Qdrant, and replaced the static 20-example block with a retrieval call that fetches k=5 examples per incoming ticket. The retrieval call adds 12 ms median latency.
Results after two weeks:
Routing accuracy increased from 71 % to 89 %.
Token cost per ticket fell 40 % (the 5 retrieved examples average 180 tokens vs. the 20-example static block at 780 tokens).
Adding a new product category required only appending ~15 labelled examples to the bank — no prompt change, no deployment.
The team added a nightly job that appended agent-verified ticket resolutions from the previous day, improving coverage incrementally.

The pipeline was instrumented to log the five retrieved example IDs alongside each response. When a routing error was caught in QA, engineers could inspect the retrieved examples to diagnose whether the error was a retrieval failure (wrong examples surfaced) or a model failure (correct examples but wrong prediction).
Common mistakes
Indexing the output instead of the input

A frequent mistake is embedding the concatenation of input and output rather than just the input. When the bank contains examples where the same input phrasing maps to different outputs, mixed embeddings create confusion in the index. Index inputs only; the output is a retrieval result, not a search dimension.

# Wrong: embeds both input and output text
vec = embed(f"{example['input']} {example['output']}")

# Correct: embed only the input
vec = embed(example['input'])
Skipping deduplication

If the bank contains near-duplicate examples, retrieval can return a cluster of nearly identical examples. The model then sees k examples that all say the same thing from slightly different angles, wasting context. Run deduplication (cosine distance threshold ~0.05) before indexing or apply MMR at retrieval time.
Ignoring ordering effects

LLMs exhibit recency bias — examples closer to the end of the context have a stronger influence. Sort retrieved examples from least to most similar (ascending), placing the most relevant example immediately before the query.

# Wrong: random or descending order
shots = retrieve(query, k=4)

# Correct: ascending similarity so the best match is last
shots = retrieve(query, k=4)
shots = shots[::-1]   # flip: least similar first, most similar last
Treating all failures as model failures

When the system produces a wrong answer, the root cause is often a retrieval failure (bad examples were selected), not a model failure. Always log retrieved example IDs alongside responses so that post-hoc debugging can distinguish the two.
Best practices
Maintain example quality gates.
Every new example should be validated for correctness before indexing. An incorrect example in the bank is worse than no example because it actively misleads the model.
Use domain-tuned embeddings.

General-purpose embedding models may fail to distinguish fine-grained domain terminology. Fine-tuning an embedding model on in-domain (query, positive, negative) triplets improves retrieval precision substantially.
Apply MMR to avoid redundant examples.
Especially useful when many bank examples are paraphrases of each other. MMR ensures the k selected examples cover diverse aspects of the query space.
Order examples ascending by similarity.
Place the most relevant example immediately before the query to exploit recency bias in attention.
Set a minimum similarity threshold.
If the best match has cosine similarity below 0.6, consider falling back to a zero-shot prompt or a smaller curated static set rather than injecting misleading examples.
Log retrieved example IDs.
Store which examples influenced each response in your observability stack. This enables example-level quality auditing and regression testing when the bank is updated.
Refresh embeddings when changing models.
Embedding spaces are not compatible across models or even across versions of the same model. A full re-index is required after any embedding model change.
Performance & scalability

ANN search latency scales sub-linearly with bank size under HNSW. Empirically, a search over 1 million vectors completes in 1–5 ms on a single CPU core with HNSW at ef_search=128. This means retrieval latency is dominated by network round-trips to an external vector store, not by the search algorithm itself.
For very high QPS systems (10 000+ requests/second), the embedding call can become the bottleneck. Strategies to address this:
Batch embed queries when possible (e.g., in a streaming queue).
Cache query embeddings using a short TTL (duplicate queries in a burst share a single embed call).
Use a lighter embedding model (smaller dimension, fewer parameters) for the retrieval path and reserve heavier models for indexing.
Shard the vector index across replicas and route queries by hash to reduce per-replica QPS.

Prompt token cost grows linearly with k (the number of retrieved examples). Profiling the marginal accuracy gain vs. token cost as k increases typically shows diminishing returns beyond k=6. Run an offline sweep over k ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8} on a held-out evaluation set to find the Pareto-optimal value for your task.
Memory usage for the FAISS index is approximately
type
bytes for a flat FP32 index, where
type
is the number of vectors and
type
is the dimension. For 1 million vectors at D=384, that is ~1.5 GB — manageable in RAM. For larger banks, use compressed indices (FAISS IVF-PQ) or an external vector database with disk-backed storage.
Tooling & ecosystem
The dynamic few-shot ecosystem covers embedding models, vector databases, and orchestration frameworks. The components are mix-and-match; no single vendor lock-in is required.
Embedding models
sentence-transformers (Hugging Face) — open-weight models (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, all-mpnet-base-v2); runs locally.
OpenAI text-embedding-3-small / text-embedding-3-large — strong general-purpose, API-only.
Cohere embed-v3 — multilingual and code variants available.
Voyage AI voyage-3 — frequently top-ranked on MTEB for retrieval tasks.
Vector databases
FAISS (Meta) — in-process, no server required; ideal for prototyping or embedding inside an existing service.
Qdrant — open-source server with filtering, payload indexing, and quantization built in.
Pinecone — managed cloud service; no infrastructure to operate.
pgvector — vector search as a PostgreSQL extension; good for teams already on Postgres.
Weaviate — graph-style schema with built-in hybrid (BM25 + vector) search.
Orchestration frameworks

# LangChain: built-in SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from langchain_core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Qdrant

selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples, embed_model, Qdrant, k=4
)

# LlamaIndex: ExampleSelector built on top of VectorStoreIndex
from llama_index.core.retrievers import VectorIndexRetriever
retriever = VectorIndexRetriever(index=vector_index, similarity_top_k=4)
nodes_with_scores = retriever.retrieve("payment failed")

Security & reliability
Prompt injection via the example bank

If user-submitted content can be added to the example bank without sanitization, an attacker can craft an input that, when retrieved as an example, injects malicious instructions into future prompts. Treat the example bank as trusted data: validate and review all entries before indexing, especially when collecting examples from production traffic.
Privacy and data leakage

Examples in the bank can contain PII or confidential content. Ensure that the retrieval system does not surface examples containing data from a different tenant in a multi-tenant deployment. Apply namespace or metadata filters scoped to the requesting user or organization.

# Tenant-scoped retrieval in Qdrant
from qdrant_client.models import Filter, FieldCondition, MatchValue

results = qdrant_client.search(
    collection_name="examples",
    query_vector=qvec,
    query_filter=Filter(must=[
        FieldCondition(key="tenant_id", match=MatchValue(value=current_tenant_id))
    ]),
    limit=5,
)
Reliability: fallback on retrieval failure

Vector store outages or latency spikes should not bring down the entire inference pipeline. Implement a fallback: if the retrieval call times out or returns too few results, fall back to a curated static few-shot set rather than failing the request. Set a hard timeout on the retrieval step (e.g., 100 ms) and handle the exception gracefully.
Index staleness

If new examples are added to the bank but the ANN index is not updated, queries for those new categories will not retrieve the new examples. Use an incremental index update strategy (FAISS supports add() on a live index; Qdrant and Pinecone support upsert) rather than rebuilding the full index on every update.
Mental model

Think of the LLM as a brilliant but context-dependent generalist. Without examples it has broad general knowledge but does not know your company's specific vocabulary, output format, or edge cases. Static few-shot is like handing it a photocopied cheat-sheet with twenty representative cases — useful, but it misses the long tail.

Dynamic few-shot is like giving the LLM access to a filing cabinet of thousands of solved cases, with a fast lookup system. For each new problem it pulls out the three or four most relevant solved cases and reviews them before answering. The filing cabinet can grow indefinitely without requiring a new cheat-sheet.
Key takeaways
Dynamic few-shot retrieves examples at inference time using embedding similarity, allowing a single LLM deployment to serve an unbounded example bank without changing the prompt template.
Example quality dominates retrieval quality. A bank of 500 well-curated examples outperforms 10 000 noisy ones.
Use cosine similarity on unit-normalized embeddings; apply Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) when the bank contains near-duplicate examples to ensure diversity.
Order retrieved examples ascending by similarity (least similar first, most similar immediately before the query) to exploit recency bias in LLM attention.
Set a fallback to static examples when retrieval times out or returns results below a minimum similarity threshold.
Log retrieved example IDs with every response to separate retrieval failures from model failures during debugging.
Re-index the entire bank when changing the embedding model — vector spaces are not compatible across models.
Token cost scales with k (examples per prompt), not with bank size; profile accuracy vs. k on a held-out set to find the Pareto-optimal trade-off.
Tenant-scope all retrieval queries in multi-tenant deployments to prevent cross-tenant data leakage through retrieved examples.
